name: Hancock, P. A.
recent_1: Gruber, M. E., Hancock, P. A., Cruit, J., & Kochan, J. A. (2026). Unraveling the Unexpected: How Pilots Can Successfully Manage Unexpected Events. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. Advance online publication. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261449578
recent_2: Hancock, P. A. (2025). Isles of autonomy: the rise of intelligent technologies. Ergonomics, 68(6), 987-996. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2447863
recent_3: Karwowski, W., Salvendy, G., Endsley, M., Rouse, W., Salmon, P., Stanney, K., ... Hancock, P. A., ... & Rau, P. (2025). Grand challenges for human factors and ergonomics. Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science, 26(4), 361-456. DOI: 10.1080/1463922X.2024.2431336
recent_1_synopsis: Examines how airline pilots cognitively and behaviorally manage unexpected, off-nominal in-flight events, drawing on case analysis and human-factors theory to identify the strategies that distinguish successful from unsuccessful responses. The work connects Hancock's long-standing interests in stress, performance under uncertainty, and aviation safety, offering implications for pilot training and flight-deck design for resilience.
recent_2_synopsis: A conceptual/critical essay introducing the 'Isles of Autonomy' metaphor to characterize how autonomous machine systems progressively penetrate the world of human work through a series of threshold events that displace human pre-eminence. It matters as a framing piece for the human-factors community on the trajectory and societal stakes of increasing machine autonomy.
recent_3_synopsis: A large multi-author position paper articulating six grand challenges for the field (e.g., societal thinking, the future of human work in Industry 5.0, climate/sustainability, education and training, personalized health, and the metaverse) along with key research thrusts. As a coauthor, Hancock contributes to an agenda-setting roadmap intended to orient HFE research priorities for the coming decade.
seminal_1: Hancock, P. A., Billings, D. R., Schaefer, K. E., Chen, J. Y. C., de Visser, E. J., & Parasuraman, R. (2011). A Meta-Analysis of Factors Affecting Trust in Human-Robot Interaction. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 53(5), 517-527. DOI: 10.1177/0018720811417254
seminal_2: Schaefer, K. E., Chen, J. Y. C., Szalma, J. L., & Hancock, P. A. (2016). A Meta-Analysis of Factors Influencing the Development of Trust in Automation: Implications for Understanding Autonomy in Future Systems. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 58(3), 377-400. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816634228
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark meta-analysis quantified how human, robot, and environmental factors shape trust in human-robot interaction, synthesizing 29 studies (69 correlational and 47 experimental effect sizes) and establishing robot performance-based attributes as the dominant trust antecedents. With roughly 1,900 citations (Semantic Scholar, June 2026), it is Hancock's most-cited work and a foundational reference that effectively defined the empirical study of trust in HRI and seeded a decade of follow-on research.
seminal_2_synopsis: A companion meta-analysis extending the trust framework to human-automation interaction, integrating human-, automation-, and environment-related factors to model how trust in automation develops, with strong implications for future autonomous systems. With roughly 575 citations (Semantic Scholar, June 2026), it is foundational for the trust-in-automation literature and is widely used to guide the design of calibrated, appropriately trusted automated systems.
expertise_overview: Hancock's research centers on human performance under stress, mental workload, fatigue, and vigilance/sustained attention, and on how these states govern performance in demanding sociotechnical settings. A major strand concerns trust in automation, robots, and AI, including the calibration of trust and human-automation/human-robot interaction, frequently studied through large-scale meta-analytic synthesis. He also has a long-standing program on human time perception and the psychology of time. His applied domains include aviation and driving safety, simulation and training (including VR/AR/MR), and the broader trajectory of machine autonomy and its consequences for human work. Methodologically he combines controlled experimentation on attention and stress with quantitative meta-analysis and conceptual/theoretical scholarship on the future of human factors and ergonomics. Recent work extends these themes to autonomous systems, pilot resilience to unexpected events, and field-defining agenda-setting on AI and human-centered design.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Peter A. Hancock, University of Central Florida (Department of Psychology / Institute for Simulation and Training). Confirmed via UCF faculty pages (peterhancock.ucf.edu, sciences.ucf.edu/psychology), PubMed, and consistency with all six pre-verified Human Factors papers. All six DOIs cited here were verified to resolve via Crossref (titles/authors/venues matched), and Hancock's authorship was confirmed in each Crossref author list: recent_1 (Gruber, Hancock, Cruit, Kochan), recent_2 (sole author Hancock), recent_3 (Hancock among 20 authors), seminal_1, and seminal_2. Citation counts are from Semantic Scholar API, retrieved 2026-06-17: HRI trust meta-analysis = 1906; trust-in-automation meta-analysis = 574; for reference, Trust in AI meta (2021) = 306 and VR/AR/MR meta (2020) = 431, so the two seminal picks are genuinely his most-cited works. NASA-TLX was checked and correctly excluded (authored by Hart & Staveland, not Hancock). Note: two other 2025 Ergonomics articles surfaced in search ('Are humans still necessary?' and 'Ergonomics and human factors: still fading') but Crossref author lists did NOT include Hancock, so they were excluded. No unverifiable citations were included.
